data.title: Lam Research saw 'impressive' share gains in Q1, BofA says; Hua Hong orders may cease
data.description:
Chip equipment spending surged in 2025, and Lam Research (LRCX) was one of the largest beneficiaries of the increase, particularly in areas like etching and process control, Bank of America said.

However, 2026 may be a slightly different story.

“On a spending category basis, etch (+22% YoY; $22.6bn) and process control (+15%; $15.1bn) outgrew overall WFE (+11% YoY; $17bn) the most, but we also flag strong litho gains (+15% YoY to $28.7bn),” analysts at the firm wrote in a note to clients released on Tuesday. “Deposition (+6%; $226bn) lagged while other categories (RTP, doping, etc.) only grew 1% YoY ($23.1bn). On a [serviceable addressable market] share basis, LRCX recorded the most impressive gains ([800 basis points year-over-year]) on etch/dep momentum, while KLAC also expanded [150 basis points year-over-year], helped by metrology progress.” 

Also seeing a gain was ASML (ASML), which had more than 60% of its serviceable addressable market. Conversely, Applied Materials (AMAT) and Tokyo Electron saw declines of 334 and 76 basis points, year-over-year, respectively. 

As such, Bank of America's analysts raised their estimates on chip equipment spending and now see the industry hitting $250B by 2030. For the broader semiconductor industry, the investment firm sees chip sales of $1.38T in 2026, up 74% year-over-year. The following year is forecast to hit $1.58T and ultimately reach $2.1T by 2030. 

“[Wafer fab equipment] intensity decreases to 11-12%, well below mid-teens % levels, but we note semi sales gains are ASP-driven (but could become unit-driven by CY29/30 as fabs come online). Further, WFE [dollar] intensity per wafer continues to secularly increase, showing LT complexity trends intact. Assuming industry reverts to historical WFE intensity by CY30, WFE could approach $300B,” the analysts added.

However, on Tuesday, Reuters reported that the Trump Administration wants chip equipment makers to stop shipments to the Hua Hong region of China.

Lam Research, ASML, Applied Materials, and KLA Corp. (KLAC) did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Ryanair CEO says fears easing about potential jet fuel shortage in Europe
data.description:
Ryanair (RYAAY) CEO Michael O’Leary said he's become less concerned about potential jet fuel shortages in Europe due to the U.S.-Iran conflict. 

“The oil companies are less concerned,” O'Leary said at the Norges Bank Investment Management conference in Norway, according to Bloomberg. “They’re now saying there’s no risk to supply disruption up to the start or middle of June.”

O'Leary noted that increased oil production from such areas as Norway, the U.S., and North Africa appears to be compensating for the disruption of shipments from the Persian Gulf. He added, however, that airline ticket prices may still increase this summer due to continued uncertainty about the ongoing conflict, Bloomberg said.

data.title: SSAB AB (publ) reports Q1 results
data.description:
• SSAB AB (publ) (SSAAF): Q1 Revenue of SEK19.16M.
  • EBITDA amounted to SEK 2,731m (2,140) corresponding to an EBITDA margin of 14.3% (13.6). 
  • EBIT increased 32% and amounted to SEK 1,920m (1,454), corresponding to an EBIT margin of 10.0% (9.2). 
  • Net income increased to SEK 1,466m (1,277) and earnings per share amounted to SEK 2.65 (2.35). 
  • Operational cash flow increased to SEK 1,017m (-14). 
  • Net liquidity amounted to SEK 3,985m compared to SEK 3,989m at year-end 2025. 
  • The AGM 2026 decided on a dividend of SEK 2.40 (2.00) per share for the financial year 2025.

data.title: GSK granted FDA priority review for Ionis-partnered hep B drug
data.description:
GSK (GSK) announced on Tuesday that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration ((FDA)) granted priority review for its marketing application for bepirovirsen, a hepatitis B therapy licensed from Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS).

The New Drug Application seeks the U.S. approval of the antisense oligonucleotide for adults with chronic hepatitis B, a viral infection causing liver disease in an estimated 1.7M people in the U.S.

The FDA has granted Breakthrough Therapy designation for the NDA, issuing October 26, 2026, as the target action date, the British drugmaker said.

The NDA is supported by data from its Phase 3 B-Well 1 and B-Well 2 trials, which met the primary endpoint, leading to statistically significant and clinically meaningful functional cure rates in patients with chronic hepatitis B.

In addition to the U.S., bepirovirsen is currently undergoing regulatory reviews in Europe, Japan, and China.

data.title: Asbury outlines full Tekion conversion by fall 2026 while maintaining about $250M in 2026 CapEx
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Asbury Automotive Group (ABG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Our first quarter results highlighted efforts to transform our business by optimizing our portfolio and successfully migrating to Tekion" (President & Director David Hult), adding, "Today, over 50% of our stores are running on Tekion" and "We remain on track and anticipate to be fully converted by the fall of this year."
  • "We divested 10 dealerships and a collision center at attractive multiples, representing approximately $600 million in annualized revenue" (President Hult), and said, "$147 million of the proceeds went towards repurchasing 678,000 shares of our stock with the rest directed towards reducing our debt."
  • "Our results reflect the expected decrease in volumes as consumer demand moderated from last year's tariff-driven spike in sales" (President Hult), while noting, "New vehicle PVRs were down just $73 sequentially" and "Used vehicle PVRs on an all-store basis was $1,847, which is up sequentially 5% and 16% year-over-year."
  • "We generated $4.1 billion in revenue" and "Our adjusted earnings per share was $5.37" (President Hult), and he added that "Parts & Service had a more challenging quarter" but "we still expect fixed operations gross profit to grow at mid-single-digit rate over time."
  • "For our financial performance in the first quarter, adjusted net income was $102 million" (Senior VP & CFO Michael Welch), and he said, "we estimate that the weather-impacted gross profit by $19 million and EPS by $0.56."
  • "Together, we have transformed our organization" (President Hult), and said, "It has been an honor and a privilege" while handing over prepared operational commentary to "our incoming Chief Executive Officer, Dan Clara" (COO & CEO Dan Clara) and adding, "I know our best days are ahead with Dan running the company."

Outlook

  • "We remain on track and anticipate to be fully converted by the fall of this year" (President Hult), while "costs related to integration and temporary disruption to store operations will also remain elevated" during the peak transition period (President Hult).
  • "It will probably peak in 3Q" for the stacked 4-to-6 month efficiency window following conversions, with "very late 2Q into 3Q" as the peak period (Senior VP & CFO Welch).
  • "We still expect fixed operations gross profit to grow at mid-single-digit rate over time" (President Hult).
  • "We also estimate the full year 2026 effective tax rate to be approximately 25%" (Senior VP & CFO Welch).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s conversion timing, management reiterated execution cadence but with higher completion percentage, as "over 50% of our stores are running on Tekion" in Q1 (President Hult) vs. "ending the year with 38 stores operating on our new DMS" in Q4 (President Hult).

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EPS was $5.37 vs. $5.62 (analysts’ Q1 2026 EPS estimate). Revenue was $4.1B vs. $4,374,696,600 (analysts’ Q1 2026 revenue estimate).
  • "Adjusted net income was $102 million" and "Adjusted EBITDA was $207 million" (Senior VP & CFO Welch), and he specified, "Adjusted net income for the first quarter of 2026 excludes" items including "net gain on divestitures of $94 million" and "$5 million related to Tekion implementation expenses" (Senior VP & CFO Welch).
  • "Adjusted SG&A as a percentage of gross profit on a same-store basis came in at 66.9%" (Senior VP & CFO Welch), and he added that results included "$2 million related to legal expenses for a specific matter" (Senior VP & CFO Welch).
  • "We generated $166 million of adjusted operating cash flow" and "Adjusted free cash flow was $120 million" (Senior VP & CFO Welch), while noting, "Excluding real estate purchases, we spent $46 million on capital expenditures" (Senior VP & CFO Welch).
  • "We ended the quarter with $1.2 billion in liquidity" and "Our transaction adjusted net leverage ratio was 3.2x" (Senior VP & CFO Welch).

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Lick, Stephens Inc.: asked for a "state of the union" into Q2 demand and the post-tax-refund backdrop; President Hult responded, "January and February were really rough for us from a weather perspective" and added, "We're definitely feeling the slowdown" and "Very difficult to predict much beyond that with what's going on with the war and gasoline prices."
  • Jeffrey Lick, Stephens Inc.: asked for a clear Tekion benefit; COO & CEO Clara answered, "gross dollars per technician being up 21%" and "average productivity per service adviser up 16%" at Koons, plus "support cost has also decreased" (COO & CEO Clara).
  • Rajat Gupta, JPMorgan: asked to split volume declines among weather, Tekion, and market; COO & CEO Clara said weather closures "affected us somewhere in the 500 car range" and described a "$13 million" same-store fixed-revenue impact, while adding on Tekion that "we close operations on that Monday" of conversion (COO & CEO Clara).
  • Rajat Gupta, JPMorgan: pressed on SG&A normalization; Senior VP & CFO Welch said, "mid-60s without the weather would have been the right number" and added Tekion efficiencies should start "in the back half of the year" (Senior VP & CFO Welch).
  • Glenn Chin, Seaport: asked whether Tekion disruption peaks in Q2; Senior VP & CFO Welch answered, "it will probably peak in 3Q" and said implementation costs in "2Q and 3Q" should be "in a similar ballpark to 1Q" (Senior VP & CFO Welch).
  • John Babcock, Barclays: asked about Herb Chambers integration and Tekion timing; COO & CEO Clara said, "Herb Chambers integration is going well" and "Tekion rollout at Chambers started last month" with Chambers "completely converted" by June (COO & CEO Clara).
  • Bret Jordan, Jefferies: asked on Stellantis; COO & CEO Clara said OEM changes are "a step in the right direction" but "it's a double-edged sword" because older inventory is "more expensive than the new model coming in" (COO & CEO Clara).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative, focusing on abnormal quarter drivers and sustainability, including "pretty noisy quarter" (Jeffrey Lick, Stephens Inc.) and requests to parse declines among "weather" and "Tekion productivity" (Rajat Gupta, JPMorgan).
  • Management tone was slightly positive but cautious, emphasizing disruption timing and uncertainty, including "Very difficult to predict much beyond that" (President Hult) and "there are some frictional costs associated with changing our DMS" (Senior VP & CFO Welch).
  • Compared with Q4, management messaging shifted from "We feel confident" on cost management during rollout (Senior VP & CFO Welch, Q4) to a more explicit timing of peak disruption, "very late 2Q into 3Q" (Senior VP & CFO Welch, Q1).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Portfolio actions accelerated, with "We divested 10 dealerships and a collision center" in Q1 (President Hult) vs. "We divested 4 stores" in Q4 (Senior VP & CFO Welch).
  • Tekion progress stepped up, with "over 50% of our stores" on Tekion in Q1 (President Hult) vs. "ending the year with 38 stores" on Tekion in Q4 (President Hult), and Q1 added clearer cadence that disruption "will probably peak in 3Q" (Senior VP & CFO Welch).
  • Analysts’ focus moved from roadmap framing in Q4 ("give us a little road map" — Jeffrey Lick, Stephens Inc.) to near-term normalization and attribution questions in Q1 ("break up how much of it was weather" — Rajat Gupta, JPMorgan).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Costs related to integration and temporary disruption to store operations will also remain elevated" during the peak conversion quarters (President Hult), and Senior VP & CFO Welch said stores are "slightly less efficient in the first 2 months" after conversion.
  • "We're definitely feeling the slowdown" (President Hult) and COO & CEO Clara cited consumer caution tied to "uncertainties" and "oil prices" (COO & CEO Clara).
  • "We still have the headwind of Stellantis" (COO & CEO Clara), and he said older-model pricing resets create "pressure to the margins" during liquidation (COO & CEO Clara).

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized a transition-heavy quarter marked by weather disruption and the peak phase of Tekion conversions, while highlighting accelerated portfolio pruning, a large Q1 buyback funded in part by divestiture proceeds, and a timeline that positions the company to "begin fully realizing the cost and efficiency benefits" after a full Tekion conversion "by the fall of this year" (President Hult).

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Celestica plummets as investors wanted more, analysts say
data.description:
Canadian technology firm Celestica (CLS) reported stronger-than-expected first-quarter results and guidance, but with shares up 50% over the past month and 380% over the past 12 months, it seems as if investors wanted more.

Shares fell 16% in midday trading on Tuesday.

“Even though the magnitude of the Q1 beat was smaller than Celestica's TTM average, the increase in FY26 guidance is more than double the increase last quarter,” RBC Capital Markets analyst Paul Treiber wrote in a note to clients.

Trieber, who has an Outperform rating and a $400 price target on Celestica, said the share price reaction likely reflects “high investor expectations for the quarter, given the 51% rally in the stock over the last month.”

J.P. Morgan analyst Samik Chatterjee had a similar take and said that even though the company raised its guidance, there are some investor concerns.

“Key questions from investors for the earnings call would relate to 1) limited upside in 1H despite stronger-than-expected Cloud spend broadly; 2) drivers relative to at least 28% H/H revenue ramp embedded in 2026 guide; and 3) incremental quantification relative to step increase implied in relation to 2027 revenue guide,” Chatterjee wrote in a note to clients.

Evercore ISI analysts said the results reinforced that AI infrastructure spending is accelerating from the hyperscalers.

“Management highlighted exceptionally strong and accelerating hyperscaler demand, with visibility into 2028, supported by long lead-time silicon, NCNR orders, and capacity alignment agreements,” the analysts wrote. “We continue to view this as confirmation of a sustained, multi-year AI capex cycle, with positive read-throughs for our covered networking names (ANET, HPE, CSCO), as outsized investments from hyperscalers should benefit multiple players.”

However, the supply constraints (largely in areas like silicon and memory), are simply an indication that any near-term upside is limited, rather than an indication of a slowdown, the analysts added.

Looking to the second quarter, Celestica expects to earn between $2.14 and $2.34 per share on an adjusted basis, above the $2.13 per share estimate. Sales are forecast to be between $4.15B and $4.45B, with the midpoint above the $4.18B estimate.

data.title: Airbus Q1 profit cut in half as aircraft deliveries slowed to lowest since 2009
data.description:
Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) down 1.6% in Tuesday's trading after reporting a sharp ​decline in Q1 core profit on Tuesday, falling well below ‌market expectations as the planemaker delivered fewer aircraft and was hurt by a weaker dollar.

Q1 adjusted EBIT plunged 52% Y/Y to €300M (~$351M), down from €624M in the year-earlier quarter and well below FactSet expectations of €378M, while sales fell 7% Y/Y to €12.65B ($14.82B), slightly above the €12.58B consensus.

Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) confirmed its full-year delivery target, saying it is on track to ship ~870 commercial aircraft to customers this year, vs. 793 it shipped in 2025 - a sign of confidence that it can assemble enough planes despite an engine shortage that forced it to slow production of its best-selling A320 jets.

The company has faulted Pratt & Whitney for failing to meet contractual obligations, leaving it at a disadvantage just as rival Boeing continues to improve production.

Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) reported cash outflow of €2.5B in the quarter, compared with a €310M drop a year earlier, because of the lower aircraft delivery and a planned buildup of inventory as it prepares for higher output.

The company had already disclosed that it delivered 114 commercial aircraft in Q1, down from 136 aircraft in the same quarter last year, meaning it would need to send ~756 units in the three remaining quarters this year to meet its goal.

data.title: American Tower raises 2026 outlook by $0.12 per share, reiterates 200-300 bps tower margin expansion target by 2030
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Tower (AMT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • American Tower opened the year by raising full-year guidance, with CEO Steven Vondran framing it as: "Our performance through the early part of the year, combined with favorable FX and straight-line dynamics led us to raise our full year outlook."
  • Vondran reiterated 2026 priorities and positioned towers and CoreSite against long-duration demand drivers, saying: "Rising wireless data consumption, accelerating cloud adoption, rapidly expanding AI-driven workloads and future generational technology shifts, all point towards sustained investment in high-quality digital infrastructure."
  • On towers and CoreSite growth assumptions, Vondran said the company is targeting "approximately 4% organic tenant billings growth across our global tower portfolio when adjusting for onetime DISH-related impacts and double-digit growth from our data center business," and described CoreSite momentum as: "this quarter marked a clear inflection in interconnection activity, enhancing both the profitability of the platform and the long-term durability of customer relationships."
  • CFO Rodney Smith summarized Q1 performance and the drivers behind the guidance increase: "our strong performance, coupled with FX and straight-line tailwinds have led us to raise our full year outlook."

Outlook

  • Smith raised full-year property revenue outlook "by approximately $145 million at the midpoint" ("approximately $110 million of FX tailwinds" and "approximately $35 million of accelerated noncash straight-line revenue in Latin America related to Oi"), while reiterating regional organic growth assumptions and full-year expectations for "organic tenant billings growth of approximately 1% or approximately 4% when excluding DISH churn and data center growth of approximately 13% year-over-year."
  • Smith raised adjusted EBITDA outlook "by approximately $105 million at the midpoint" and raised attributable AFFO outlook "by $0.12 per share," adding that attributable AFFO per share growth on an FX-neutral basis is expected to be "faster in the back half of the year than the front half" due to maintenance capital and cash tax timing.
  • Longer-term growth framing in Q&A centered on AFFO durability after event-driven items, with Smith stating: "The outlook for 2026 is in line with our longer-term view for AFFO per share growth, which is up in the mid-single digits to better than mid-single digits before you account for the impacts of FX and interest rates."

Financial Results

  • Smith said consolidated property revenue grew "approximately 3% year-over-year when excluding noncash straight-line revenue and FX impacts," and on a DISH-normalized basis "property revenue grew approximately 5% on a cash FX-neutral basis," driven by "organic tenant billings growth of approximately 2% or 4% normalized for the impact of onetime DISH churn" and "data center cash revenue growth of approximately 17%."
  • Smith reported profitability trends as: "Adjusted EBITDA grew 1% when excluding net straight-line and FX impacts," while "cash adjusted EBITDA margins declined approximately 110 basis points year-over-year, primarily due to DISH-related churn, SG&A timing and higher fuel prices in Africa."
  • By region, Smith cited organic tenant billings growth of "approximately 1%" in the U.S. and Canada ("approximately 5% when excluding DISH churn"), "approximately 11%" in Africa and APAC, "approximately 4%" in Europe, and Latin America down "approximately 2%, primarily driven by elevated churn in Brazil."

Q&A

  • Ric Prentiss, Raymond James: asked about EchoStar DISH/AT&T spectrum deal pace and whether an escrow path is being discussed; CEO Vondran responded: "We really can't comment on ongoing litigation" and added, "we believe our contract is enforceable" and "we've completely derisked our earnings and our guidance by taking DISH out of our numbers."
  • Ric Prentiss, Raymond James: asked about Europe new builds, contract structure, and returns; CFO Smith said Europe has "outperform[ed] the original business case" and expects returns "above our weighted average cost of capital in that region by a couple of hundred basis points over time," while Vondran added: "we are also winning some things that are outside the contract on very good terms because of our operational excellence."
  • Michael Rollins, Citi: asked how AMT is approaching M&A and whether it would consider U.S. public-to-public or public-to-private; Vondran said, "We have a very disciplined capital allocation formula" and "There's no sort of strategic imperative to overpay for anything." Rollins also asked about AFFO expectations beyond 2026; Smith pointed to "mid-single digits to upper single-digit AFFO and AFFO per share growth rate over time."
  • Eric Luebchow, Wells Fargo: asked about edge compute and whether CoreSite belongs in AMT longer term; Vondran said, "edge is absolutely something that we think is going to continue to grow" and added, "I do think it has a long-term place in our portfolio."
  • Cameron McVeigh, Morgan Stanley: asked about expanding vs. retrofitting CoreSite amid power and supply constraints; Vondran said the team now takes a "longer-term view" on land/power/components and is "being more aggressive," while Smith quantified capacity planning: CoreSite development held for development increased from "about 287 -- 280 megawatts" by "200 megawatts."
  • Brendan Lynch, Barclays: asked about Brazil normalization path and churn/new business; Smith said Latin America churn is "peaking" and expects organic tenant billings to move "from a negative OTBG into positive territory in the lower single digits in '27" with "normalized growth" by "'28 and beyond," while Vondran added: "We're absolutely seeing an increase in demand" in Brazil.
  • David Barden, New Street: asked about implications of rumored SBA privatization and how satellites could disrupt terrestrial networks; Vondran said, "we're not going to comment on the rumors" and on satellites: "Satellites are complementary to terrestrial networks" and "I don't lose a second sleep worried about satellites."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on headline risks and valuation cross-currents, with repeated probing on DISH uncertainty, private-market multiples, and potential disruption (e.g., Prentiss citing DISH process concerns and Barden asking, "What does it mean if SBA gets taken private?").
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded in legal-related Q&A, with firm repetition of de-risking language (Vondran: "we've completely derisked our earnings and our guidance") and confidence on strategy and CoreSite demand (Vondran: "The business has meaningfully exceeded our expectations").
  • Versus last quarter, Q1 maintained confidence but became more restrictive on DISH commentary; in Q4, Vondran emphasized the run-rate reset while still providing directional exposure context, whereas in Q1 he said, "we really can't comment" and leaned on the public docket.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from establishing a DISH-reset baseline in Q4 2025 to formally raising consolidated outlook in Q1 2026, with Smith attributing the increase to "incremental FX and straight-line tailwinds."
  • CoreSite narrative strengthened from Q4’s emphasis on "impressive double-digit growth" and AI-ready capacity to Q1’s "clear inflection in interconnection activity" and explicit willingness to "accelerat[e] CoreSite's expansion" as a larger value driver.
  • Analysts stayed focused on DISH and capital allocation in both quarters, but Q1 added heavier questioning on private-market valuation signals (SBA/take-private framing) and disruption narratives (SpaceX/satellites).

Risks and Concerns

  • DISH remains an overhang primarily through litigation uncertainty; Vondran said, "we really can't comment on ongoing litigation" but emphasized: "we've completely derisked our earnings and our guidance by taking DISH out of our numbers."
  • Latin America churn pressure, especially Brazil, continued to weigh on organic growth; Smith attributed the 2026 pattern to churn timing shifts and pointed to a recovery path into 2027 and normalized growth by 2028.
  • CoreSite development faces local permitting resistance; Vondran acknowledged, "we are seeing an increase" in NIMBY-related friction, while adding it has not forced project cancellations or "significant delays" so far.

Final Takeaway

Management’s Q1 message centered on a raised 2026 outlook driven mainly by FX and straight-line tailwinds, continued DISH de-risking with any DISH recovery framed as incremental upside, and an increasingly prominent CoreSite thesis built around accelerating AI-driven workloads and an interconnection inflection. Across Q&A, investors pressed on litigation opacity, capital allocation in a shifting public/private valuation landscape, Latin America churn timing, and disruption risk, while management leaned on disciplined return hurdles, balance-sheet flexibility, and the view that towers and CoreSite remain positioned for sustained demand.
